subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 11. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your role is to talk, show, fold, cut and share alongside the child, using the simple scripts and activities in this guide. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 1 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Say that a half is one of two equal parts of a whole.
Understand that the two halves must be equal in size or amount.
Tell the difference between equal parts and unequal parts.
Fold or draw a line to cut a shape into two halves.
See that a shape can often be halved in more than one way.
Put two halves back together to remake the whole.
Share a small group of objects equally between two to find half of a set.
Find half of small even numbers: half of 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10.
Recognise and say the symbol ½ as ‘one half’.

concepts.title: What a Half Really Means (two equal parts)
concepts.explanation: The whole unit rests on one idea: a half is one of two equal parts. Not just two parts — two equal parts. A child will happily break a biscuit into a big bit and a tiny bit and call them ‘halves’, because there are two pieces. Your job all week is to keep returning to the word equal: the two pieces must be the same size, so that it is fair. How to teach it: Take one piece of anjero (or a paper circle) and say, ‘This is one whole.’ Fold it carefully down the middle so the two sides sit exactly on top of each other,…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “I have one whole anjero. Watch me fold it. Do the two sides match?” Child: “Yes, they are the same.” You: “So each part is a half. Half plus half makes one whole.” You (tearing a big bit and…

concepts.title: Halves of Shapes (fold, cut and draw a line)
concepts.explanation: Now the child halves shapes. The key surprise for them is that a shape can often be cut in half in more than one way, and every correct way still makes two equal parts. A circle can be halved with a line straight down, straight across, or slanted — as long as the line goes through the middle. A square or rectangle can be halved down the middle the tall way, the wide way, or corner to corner. How to teach it: Folding is the child's best friend here, because a fold that matches exactly proves the two parts are…
concepts.example: One idea gently stretches the child: the two halves must be equal in size, but they do not have to be the same shape to look at. If you cut a square from corner to corner you get two triangles; they look different from…

concepts.title: Half of a Set (share a group equally between two)
concepts.explanation: A half is not only for shapes — it works for groups of objects too. To find half of a set, the child shares the group equally between two. Each of the two groups then holds the same number, and that number is ‘half of’ the whole set. This is where halves connect to counting, which the child already knows well. How to teach it: Put out 6 dates. Give the child two plates. ‘Share the dates fairly — one for this plate, one for that plate, one for this plate…’ Deal them out one at a time, back and forth, until they are…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Here are 8 stones and two cloths. Let us share them fairly between two.” You and child (dealing): “One for you, one for me, one for you, one for me…” You: “Now count one group. How many?”…

concepts.title: Halving Small Even Numbers
concepts.explanation: From sharing real objects, the child moves to halving numbers: half of 2 is 1, half of 4 is 2, half of 6 is 3, half of 8 is 4, half of 10 is 5. Keep to even numbers only for now, because only even numbers share exactly into two equal groups. Do not rush to the abstract number — always let the child reach the answer by dealing real counters first, then say the number sentence. How to teach it: Connect halving to doubling, which is the same fact seen from the other side. ‘Half of 8 is 4 — and look, 4 and 4 make 8.’…
concepts.example: You can also work backwards, which children enjoy as a puzzle: ‘Half of what number is 5?’ Deal 5 stones onto each of two plates, push them together, count 10 — ‘half of 10 is 5.’ Moving comfortably in both directions,…

concepts.title: The Symbol ½ and Everyday Halves
concepts.explanation: Finally, show the written symbol ½ and say ‘one half’ each time the child sees it. Explain simply that the little line means the whole has been split into equal parts. Do not expect the child to write ½ neatly — recognising it and reading it as ‘one half’ is plenty for Year 1. Always tie the symbol back to a picture or an object, so ½ means something real and not just marks on a page. Point out halves in everyday life so the idea lives beyond the lesson. ‘Half past four, when we go to the masjid’ — a half of the…
concepts.example: ‘Any two pieces are halves.’ The most common error — the child ignores size. Always ask ‘Are the two parts equal?’ Fold or match them to check.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

terms: The complete thing before it is cut or shared
One of two equal parts of a whole
Exactly the same size or amount
Not the same size — so not halves
The symbol we write for one half
Give the same amount to each of two
The amount in one of two equal groups

questions.question: What does “Whole” mean?
options: The complete thing before it is cut or shared
One of two equal parts of a whole
Exactly the same size or amount
Not the same size — so not halves
questions.answer: The complete thing before it is cut or shared
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Whole means The complete thing before it is cut or shared.

questions.question: What does “Half” mean?
options: One of two equal parts of a whole
Exactly the same size or amount
Not the same size — so not halves
questions.answer: One of two equal parts of a whole
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Half means One of two equal parts of a whole.

questions.question: What does “Equal / the same as” mean?
options: Exactly the same size or amount
Not the same size — so not halves
The symbol we write for one half
questions.answer: Exactly the same size or amount
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Equal / the same as means Exactly the same size or amount.

questions.question: What does “Unequal” mean?
options: Not the same size — so not halves
The symbol we write for one half
Give the same amount to each of two
questions.answer: Not the same size — so not halves
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Unequal means Not the same size — so not halves.

questions.question: What does “Fraction” mean?
options: The symbol we write for one half
Give the same amount to each of two
The amount in one of two equal groups
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Fraction means A part of a whole.

selfAssessment: I can say that a half is one of two equal parts of a whole.
I can understand that the two halves must be equal in size or amount.
I can tell the difference between equal parts and unequal parts.
I can fold or draw a line to cut a shape into two halves.
I can see that a shape can often be halved in more than one way.
I can put two halves back together to remake the whole.
I can share a small group of objects equally between two to find half of a set.
I can find half of small even numbers: half of 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10.
